title: Perianal Abscess & Fistula
oneLiner: infected anal gland causing perianal pain/swelling; requires prompt drainage to avoid sepsis & chronic fistula tract.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Fournier’s Gangrene (necrotising fasciitis of perineum):** crepitus on palpation, rapid skin necrosis, systemic toxicity (high NEWS2, lactate >2, altered mental state) – **immediate surgical referral + antibiotics + ICU**
- **Septic shock from delayed drainage:** hypotension, tachycardia, fever >38.5°C, lactate >4 — **fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, urgent I&D**
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe, constant, throbbing perianal pain (worse sitting, walking, defecating)
  - Localised swelling, redness, warmth
  - Purulent discharge (if pointing or spontaneously ruptured)
  - Fever, malaise, rigors (if systemic spread)
  - In chronic fistula: intermittent discharge of pus/blood from external opening, recurrent abscesses
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Inspection: erythematous, tense, fluctuant mass near anus; external opening (for fistula); may be posterior, anterior, or lateral
  - Palpation: extreme tenderness, induration, fluctuance (if abscess); firm cord (fistula tract)
  - Digital rectal exam (DRE): avoid if too painful without anaesthesia; may feel internal opening (if fistula)
  - Note: always examine surrounding areas (scrotum/labia, natal cleft) for extension
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Perianal abscess:** fluctuant mass, acute pain, systemic signs
- **Fistula-in-ano:** persistent external opening, intermittent discharge, history of prior abscess
- **Pilonidal sinus:** midline natal cleft, not perianal; contains hair tufts
- **Hidradenitis suppurativa:** chronic inflammatory condition of axillae, groin; multiple sinuses, scarring, "double comedones"
- **Crohn’s disease fistula:** often painless, "snail-track" ulcers, multiple/atypical locations (anterior, bilateral), associated GI symptoms (diarrhoea, weight loss)
- **Anal fissure:** sharp pain on defecation, blood on paper; posterior midline; no fluctuance
- **Ischiorectal/intersphincteric abscess:** deep pain, no external fluctuation; may present with sepsis without perianal swelling

**Splitter:** *Fluctuance + tenderness + acute onset* → abscess vs *chronic external opening with intermittent discharge* → fistula. For Crohn’s vs cryptoglandular: **multiple, painless, bilateral fistulas** strongly suggest Crohn's.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - DRE (if tolerated) – assess tenderness, internal opening
  - Check for crepitus (Fournier’s)
  - Record NEWS2, lactate (capillary point-of-care if available)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, U&Es, lactate
  - Blood cultures if febrile/systemic
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None needed for typical superficial abscess – proceed to drainage
  - **Second-line (if deep/complex/failure to drain):** MRI pelvis with contrast (gold standard for fistula mapping); CT for deep abscess or suspected Fournier's
- **Special tests:**
  - Goodall’s rule (for fistula): if opening is anterior to a line drawn through the ischial tuberosities → fistulas tract straight; if posterior → curved tract
  - Anorectal manometry (if surgery planned for complex fistula)
- **Never-miss test:** **Examination for crepitus + systemic markers (NEWS2, lactate)** – if present, action immediately as Fournier’s → CT + surgical consult (do not wait for MRI)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Incision & drainage (I&D)** – under local anaesthesia (1% lidocaine with adrenaline) if small/accessible; GA if large/deep or patient unable to tolerate
  - Send pus for microbiology (MC+S)
  - IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS) only if: immunocompromised, severe cellulitis, systemic sepsis, prosthetic valve/device
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g IV/PO + NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen 400mg PO) if no contraindications; consider IV morphine for severe pain
  - Fluid resuscitation if hypotensive (IV 0.9% sodium chloride 500ml stat)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Daily wound examination: check for spreading cellulitis, delayed healing
  - Warm sitz baths (saline or plain water) 3-4 times daily after bowel movements
  - If fistula suspected post-drainage: refer colorectal surgery outpatient for MRI + elective fistulotomy/seton
  - Antibiotics adjustment per sensitivities (if sent; step down to oral if improving)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Abscess: review in surgical clinic in 2 weeks; if persistent discharge, assess for fistula
  - Fistula: elective fistulotomy (low fistulas; risk of incontinence <5%) or seton insertion (high/ complex)
  - Consider Crohn’s disease if multiple/atypical fistulas – refer gastroenterology
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h (then reassess); NBM if for theatre (or light diet if not)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (unless high bleeding risk)
  - Observations: NEWS2 q4h (q1h if sepsis)
  - Wound care: daily inspection; pack loosely if open cavity
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line antibiotic (if needed):**
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 625 mg PO TDS (mild/moderate) OR 1.2 g IV TDS (severe) – [check local policy for alternatives]
  - **Alternative (penicillin allergy):** Metronidazole 400 mg PO TDS + Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD (or Cephalexin 500 mg PO QDS if not allergic to cephalosporins)
- **Duration:** 5-7 days (oral) if cellulitis; stop if no infection after drainage
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO QDS; Ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (with food); Oramorph 5-10 mg PO PRN
- **Key interactions:** Co-amoxiclav ↔ methotrexate ↑ toxicity; NSAIDs ↔ anticoagulants ↑ bleed risk
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: adjust dose if eGFR <30 (dosing interval every 12h); hepatic impairment – monitor LFTs
  - Metronidazole: reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving a stat dose of any antibiotic before drainage – never rely on antibiotics alone to treat an abscess (except in Fournier’s where urgent antibiotics are given before surgery)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Sepsis (gram-negative anaerobes)
  - Fournier’s gangrene (rapidly fatal if missed)
  - Failure of resolution → recurrent abscess
  - Extension into ischiorectal/supralevator space
- **Chronic:**
  - Fistula formation (30-50% after abscess)
  - Incontinence (post-fistulotomy – risk higher in anterior fistulas, female, high tracts)
  - Recurrence (if seton used; underlying IBD)
  - Anal stricture (rare)
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality near-zero after simple drainage (but significant if Fournier’s – up to 40%)
- Abscess recurrence ~10% in 5 years; fistula recurrence after fistulotomy ~5-10%
- Most patients discharged same-day after I&D; fistula management is elective
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document external opening location and use Goodsall’s rule** to predict tract direction – helps surgeon planning fistulotomy.
- **If patient has Crohn’s disease, never proceed with fistulotomy without MRI + gastroenterology input** – risk of incontinence and poor wound healing.
- **In a septic patient with perianal pain but no fluctuance, consider deep intersphincteric/ischiorectal abscess** – arrange CT and discuss with colorectal surgeon immediately.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT prescribe oral antibiotics alone for a discrete abscess** – this delays drainage and risks sepsis; drainage is definitive treatment.
- **Do NOT assume all perianal fistulas are cryptoglandular** – in young patients with painless, multiple, or bilateral fistulas, rule out Crohn’s (ask about diarrhoea, weight loss, family history).
- **Do NOT perform a DRE in a patient with suspected Fournier’s** – it may cause pain and spread infection; proceed to CT/surgical team.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Increasing pain, fever >38°C, inability to pass urine, spreading redness/cellulitis, new crepitus (crackling under skin), or wound not healing after 3-5 days.
- **Follow-up:** Surgical outpatients in 2 weeks (abscess) or 4-6 weeks (fistula) for elective workup.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or any single parameter red (e.g., RR >24, HR >120, SBP <90) – review by senior clinician within 1 hour
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** – repeat after fluid bolus; if >4 → consider sepsis + ICU
- **Suspicion of Fournier’s (crepitus, rapid spread, systemic toxicity)** – immediate surgical referral + CT and commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV + metronidazole 500mg IV, per local policy) – inform ICU
- **Inability to tolerate I&D in ED** – request GA/surgical theatre booking

rawMarkdown:
## Perianal Abscess & Fistula

**One-liner:** infected anal gland causing perianal pain/swelling; requires prompt drainage to avoid sepsis & chronic fistula tract.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Fournier’s Gangrene (necrotising fasciitis of perineum):** crepitus on palpation, rapid skin necrosis, systemic toxicity (high NEWS2, lactate >2, altered mental state) – **immediate surgical referral + antibiotics + ICU**
- **Septic shock from delayed drainage:** hypotension, tachycardia, fever >38.5°C, lactate >4 — **fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, urgent I&D**

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe, constant, throbbing perianal pain (worse sitting, walking, defecating)
  - Localised swelling, redness, warmth
  - Purulent discharge (if pointing or spontaneously ruptured)
  - Fever, malaise, rigors (if systemic spread)
  - In chronic fistula: intermittent discharge of pus/blood from external opening, recurrent abscesses
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Inspection: erythematous, tense, fluctuant mass near anus; external opening (for fistula); may be posterior, anterior, or lateral
  - Palpation: extreme tenderness, induration, fluctuance (if abscess); firm cord (fistula tract)
  - Digital rectal exam (DRE): avoid if too painful without anaesthesia; may feel internal opening (if fistula)
  - Note: always examine surrounding areas (scrotum/labia, natal cleft) for extension

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Perianal abscess:** fluctuant mass, acute pain, systemic signs
- **Fistula-in-ano:** persistent external opening, intermittent discharge, history of prior abscess
- **Pilonidal sinus:** midline natal cleft, not perianal; contains hair tufts
- **Hidradenitis suppurativa:** chronic inflammatory condition of axillae, groin; multiple sinuses, scarring, "double comedones"
- **Crohn’s disease fistula:** often painless, "snail-track" ulcers, multiple/atypical locations (anterior, bilateral), associated GI symptoms (diarrhoea, weight loss)
- **Anal fissure:** sharp pain on defecation, blood on paper; posterior midline; no fluctuance
- **Ischiorectal/intersphincteric abscess:** deep pain, no external fluctuation; may present with sepsis without perianal swelling

**Splitter:** *Fluctuance + tenderness + acute onset* → abscess vs *chronic external opening with intermittent discharge* → fistula. For Crohn’s vs cryptoglandular: **multiple, painless, bilateral fistulas** strongly suggest Crohn's.

### Investigations

- **Bedside:**
  - DRE (if tolerated) – assess tenderness, internal opening
  - Check for crepitus (Fournier’s)
  - Record NEWS2, lactate (capillary point-of-care if available)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, U&Es, lactate
  - Blood cultures if febrile/systemic
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None needed for typical superficial abscess – proceed to drainage
  - **Second-line (if deep/complex/failure to drain):** MRI pelvis with contrast (gold standard for fistula mapping); CT for deep abscess or suspected Fournier's
- **Special tests:**
  - Goodall’s rule (for fistula): if opening is anterior to a line drawn through the ischial tuberosities → fistulas tract straight; if posterior → curved tract
  - Anorectal manometry (if surgery planned for complex fistula)
- **Never-miss test:** **Examination for crepitus + systemic markers (NEWS2, lactate)** – if present, action immediately as Fournier’s → CT + surgical consult (do not wait for MRI)

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Incision & drainage (I&D)** – under local anaesthesia (1% lidocaine with adrenaline) if small/accessible; GA if large/deep or patient unable to tolerate
  - Send pus for microbiology (MC+S)
  - IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS) only if: immunocompromised, severe cellulitis, systemic sepsis, prosthetic valve/device
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g IV/PO + NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen 400mg PO) if no contraindications; consider IV morphine for severe pain
  - Fluid resuscitation if hypotensive (IV 0.9% sodium chloride 500ml stat)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Daily wound examination: check for spreading cellulitis, delayed healing
  - Warm sitz baths (saline or plain water) 3-4 times daily after bowel movements
  - If fistula suspected post-drainage: refer colorectal surgery outpatient for MRI + elective fistulotomy/seton
  - Antibiotics adjustment per sensitivities (if sent; step down to oral if improving)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Abscess: review in surgical clinic in 2 weeks; if persistent discharge, assess for fistula
  - Fistula: elective fistulotomy (low fistulas; risk of incontinence <5%) or seton insertion (high/ complex)
  - Consider Crohn’s disease if multiple/atypical fistulas – refer gastroenterology
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h (then reassess); NBM if for theatre (or light diet if not)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (unless high bleeding risk)
  - Observations: NEWS2 q4h (q1h if sepsis)
  - Wound care: daily inspection; pack loosely if open cavity

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line antibiotic (if needed):**
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 625 mg PO TDS (mild/moderate) OR 1.2 g IV TDS (severe) – [check local policy for alternatives]
  - **Alternative (penicillin allergy):** Metronidazole 400 mg PO TDS + Doxycycline 100 mg PO BD (or Cephalexin 500 mg PO QDS if not allergic to cephalosporins)
- **Duration:** 5-7 days (oral) if cellulitis; stop if no infection after drainage
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO QDS; Ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (with food); Oramorph 5-10 mg PO PRN
- **Key interactions:** Co-amoxiclav ↔ methotrexate ↑ toxicity; NSAIDs ↔ anticoagulants ↑ bleed risk
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: adjust dose if eGFR <30 (dosing interval every 12h); hepatic impairment – monitor LFTs
  - Metronidazole: reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving a stat dose of any antibiotic before drainage – never rely on antibiotics alone to treat an abscess (except in Fournier’s where urgent antibiotics are given before surgery)

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Sepsis (gram-negative anaerobes)
  - Fournier’s gangrene (rapidly fatal if missed)
  - Failure of resolution → recurrent abscess
  - Extension into ischiorectal/supralevator space
- **Chronic:**
  - Fistula formation (30-50% after abscess)
  - Incontinence (post-fistulotomy – risk higher in anterior fistulas, female, high tracts)
  - Recurrence (if seton used; underlying IBD)
  - Anal stricture (rare)

### Prognosis

- Mortality near-zero after simple drainage (but significant if Fournier’s – up to 40%)
- Abscess recurrence ~10% in 5 years; fistula recurrence after fistulotomy ~5-10%
- Most patients discharged same-day after I&D; fistula management is elective

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always document external opening location and use Goodsall’s rule** to predict tract direction – helps surgeon planning fistulotomy.
- **If patient has Crohn’s disease, never proceed with fistulotomy without MRI + gastroenterology input** – risk of incontinence and poor wound healing.
- **In a septic patient with perianal pain but no fluctuance, consider deep intersphincteric/ischiorectal abscess** – arrange CT and discuss with colorectal surgeon immediately.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do NOT prescribe oral antibiotics alone for a discrete abscess** – this delays drainage and risks sepsis; drainage is definitive treatment.
- **Do NOT assume all perianal fistulas are cryptoglandular** – in young patients with painless, multiple, or bilateral fistulas, rule out Crohn’s (ask about diarrhoea, weight loss, family history).
- **Do NOT perform a DRE in a patient with suspected Fournier’s** – it may cause pain and spread infection; proceed to CT/surgical team.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:** Increasing pain, fever >38°C, inability to pass urine, spreading redness/cellulitis, new crepitus (crackling under skin), or wound not healing after 3-5 days.
- **Follow-up:** Surgical outpatients in 2 weeks (abscess) or 4-6 weeks (fistula) for elective workup.

### Escalation Criteria

- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or any single parameter red (e.g., RR >24, HR >120, SBP <90) – review by senior clinician within 1 hour
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** – repeat after fluid bolus; if >4 → consider sepsis + ICU
- **Suspicion of Fournier’s (crepitus, rapid spread, systemic toxicity)** – immediate surgical referral + CT and commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV + metronidazole 500mg IV, per local policy) – inform ICU
- **Inability to tolerate I&D in ED** – request GA/surgical theatre booking
